nodes.title: PDE4A10 Docking and Pocket Mapping
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Human PDE4A10 structure (PDB 2QYK) was prepared in Schrödinger, missing side chains and loops were refined, protonation states were assigned near physiological pH, and SiteMap was used to rank candidate pockets for druggability. Compound 11h was prepared with LigPrep and docked across five predicted sites using Glide SP. The work established Site 1 as highly druggable and identified Site 3 as the most favorable docking environment for 11h, with hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions proposed to explain binding.

nodes.title: PDE4 Isoform cAMP Inhibition Assay
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. A luminescence-based cAMP assay was used to quantify inhibition of PDE4A1, PDE4B2, PDE4C1, PDE4D2, and PDE4D3 by 11h. Duplicate dose-response curves were fitted with a four-parameter logistic model to determine IC50 values. The study established broad, low-nanomolar PDE4 inhibition, with particularly strong activity against PDE4D splice variants relevant to CNS pharmacology.

nodes.title: RAW264.7 Macrophage Inflammation Assay
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. RAW 264.7 murine macrophage-like cells were pretreated with 11h over a concentration range and then challenged with LPS. TNFalpha and nitrite release into supernatant were quantified while cell viability was monitored in parallel. The experiment showed dose-dependent suppression of inflammatory mediators without measurable cytotoxicity.

nodes.title: IMG Microglia-Like Cell Inflammation Assay
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Immortalized murine microglia-like IMG cells were pretreated with 11h and challenged with LPS. TNFalpha and IL-6 were measured in culture supernatants and viability was quantified in matched wells. The experiment demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in a microglial model relevant to neuroinflammation while preserving viability.

nodes.title: Human PBMC LPS/TNFalpha Assay
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Human PBMCs were stimulated with LPS in the presence or absence of 11h across a broad concentration range. Supernatants were collected after 24 hours and TNFalpha was quantified by ELISA. This experiment extended the anti-inflammatory signal from murine cell systems into a human primary immune cell context.

nodes.title: Mouse LPS Neuroinflammation Flow Cytometry
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. C57BL/6 mice received subacute LPS, then were dosed with 11h and brains were collected 48 hours later for single-cell immunophenotyping. Flow cytometry quantified M1 and M2 macrophage markers, TLR4-positive cells, TNFalpha, IFNgamma, IL-6, PGE2, nitrites, and CD4/CD8 T-cell populations. The experiment established in vivo neuroimmune modulation after acute inflammatory challenge.

nodes.title: Mouse Brain RT-qPCR for MyD88 Pathway
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Brain tissue from the same LPS neuroinflammation study was analyzed by RT-qPCR for CXCL10, TNFalpha, NFkB, MYD88, IL1R1, FBL, and TRAAD transcripts. The study supported the hypothesis that 11h suppresses MyD88-linked inflammatory signaling downstream of PDE4 inhibition.

nodes.title: CYP450 Inhibition Panel
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. 11h was tested against six major human CYP450 isoforms at screening concentrations followed by IC50 follow-up where needed. The panel was designed to assess metabolic interaction liability for chronic CNS dosing. The outcome indicated minimal clinically meaningful CYP inhibition risk at expected therapeutic exposures.

nodes.title: Cardiac Ion Channel CiPA/QPatch Panel
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Automated patch clamp assays profiled 11h against hERG, Cav1.2, and Nav1.5 over a concentration range. The goal was to derisk proarrhythmic liability early in development. No material inhibition was observed in the tested range, supporting low primary cardiac ion channel concern.

nodes.title: Non-GLP Ames Mutagenicity Screen
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. 11h was tested in Salmonella strains TA98, TA100, TA1535, and TA1537 with and without S9 activation over a broad concentration range, with bacterial cytotoxicity monitored in parallel. The package showed no convincing mutagenicity signal and no cytotoxicity, though one weak isolated signal was noted in TA1537 plus S9 and not corroborated elsewhere.

nodes.title: Off-Target Binding and Enzyme Profiling
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. 11h was screened across a broad panel of neurologically relevant receptors, transporters, ion channels, and enzymes. The work was intended to identify alternate mechanisms or adverse-effect liabilities. Only weak high-concentration binding was seen for A2A and progesterone receptors, with no major off-target concern emerging from the screen.

nodes.title: Single-Dose Rat PK IV/PO Study
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received 11h by IV and oral routes, and serial plasma samples were collected for LC-MS/MS analysis. The objective was to estimate exposure, half-life, clearance, oral bioavailability, and dose proportionality. The study established oral exposure and supported brain-penetrant development despite relatively low oral bioavailability.

nodes.title: Five-Day Repeat-Dose Rat PK Study
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Rats received oral 11h once daily for five days and plasma PK was characterized on day 5. The study examined accumulation, Cmax, AUC, and whether once-daily dosing could maintain exposure. The reported increase in systemic exposure on day 5 supported repeat-dose feasibility.

nodes.title: Rat Brain and Tissue Distribution Study
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. After oral dosing, brain, plasma, and peripheral tissues were collected at pre-Cmax, 24-hour, and 48-hour time points and quantified by LC-MS/MS. The experiment directly measured brain penetration and persistence relative to plasma. Sustained brain concentrations above plasma were one of the key translational findings in the paper.

nodes.title: Red Blood Cell Partitioning Assay
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. 11h was incubated in whole blood and plasma concentrations were compared to calculate blood-to-plasma partitioning. The goal was to understand whether unusual blood cell sequestration could distort PK interpretation. The assay indicated limited red blood cell partitioning across species.

nodes.title: Mouse Maximum Tolerated Dose Study
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Mice received escalating single oral doses up to 40 mg/kg and were monitored for mortality, weight loss, food intake, grooming, and overt clinical signs over seven days. No maximum tolerated dose was reached under the tested conditions, which supported continued development and widened the initial tolerability margin.

nodes.title: 28-Day Rat Repeat-Dose Tolerability
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Rats received daily 11h intraperitoneally for 28 days and were monitored longitudinally for weight and overt behavioral changes. The study was not a GLP toxicology study but provided an early repeat-dose tolerability signal and showed only transient body weight effects without persistent clinical concerns.

nodes.title: Ferret Emesis and TNFalpha PD Study
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Ferrets received single oral doses of 11h or ibudilast and were observed for retching, vomiting, salivation, and related behaviors over two hours, after which blood TNFalpha was measured. The study directly addressed the historical emesis liability of PDE4 inhibitors and suggested that 11h retained pharmacodynamic activity at non-emetic doses.

nodes.title: Rat Elevated Plus and Zero Maze Study
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Rats received repeat dosing with 11h, vehicle, or rimonabant and were tested in the elevated plus maze and elevated zero maze. The work aimed to identify anxiogenic or other adverse neurobehavioral effects while also exploring beneficial CNS behavioral signals. 11h showed no evidence of the adverse behavioral pattern seen with rimonabant.

nodes.title: Rat Forced Swim Test
nodes.content: Completed experiment extracted from the paper. Rats received subchronic dosing with 11h and were then evaluated in the forced swim test to assess immobility. The experiment was used both as a behavioral safety readout and as an early signal for antidepressant-like activity. Reduced immobility was reported after 11h treatment.

nodes.title: Integrated Candidate Review and IND Gap Assessment
nodes.content: Future experiment and program gate. Assemble all paper data into a formal candidate review package that explicitly scores 11h on pharmacology, translational PK, tolerability, known liabilities of the PDE4 class, and remaining IND-enabling gaps. The output should be a cross-functional decision that 11h is the development candidate, a ranked risk register, and a written list of studies still required under ICH and FDA expectations for a first-in-human IND.

nodes.title: Target Product Profile and Initial Clinical Positioning
nodes.content: Future planning node. Define the initial clinical indication, route of administration, dosing intent, patient population, and the efficacy and safety claims that the nonclinical package must support. The team should write a target product profile for an orally available CNS-active PDE4 inhibitor and specify the minimum data package needed to justify single-ascending-dose entry into humans.

nodes.title: API Process Development and Route Selection
nodes.content: Future CMC experiment. Develop a scalable, impurity-controlled synthetic route for 11h that can support toxicology and later GMP manufacture. The study should compare the medicinal chemistry route against at least one scale-up alternative, identify critical reagents and potential genotoxic or carryover impurities, establish a control strategy for key intermediates, and produce material suitable for formulation and analytical method development.

nodes.title: Analytical Methods, Reference Standards, and Impurity Profiling
nodes.content: Future CMC/analytical experiment. Build phase-appropriate analytical methods for identity, assay, purity, residual solvents, and degradation products for 11h and key related substances. The work should generate a qualified reference standard, orthogonal purity methods, and an impurity risk assessment that can support release testing for tox lots and later GMP lots.

nodes.title: Salt, Polymorph, and Solid-State Developability Screen
nodes.content: Future CMC experiment. Characterize free base and feasible salt forms, assess polymorphism, hygroscopicity, thermal behavior, particle properties, and crystallinity, and decide what physical form should be taken into toxicology and clinical formulation. This node matters because changes in physical form later could alter exposure and undermine interpretability of tox or first-in-human data.

nodes.title: Prototype Oral Formulation and Vehicle Bridging
nodes.content: Future formulation experiment. Build a toxicology-ready oral formulation with acceptable content uniformity, dose suspension or dosage-form stability, and exposure reproducibility in the intended preclinical species. The study should compare at least two formulation approaches, bridge them against the paper vehicle where relevant, and choose a formulation strategy that can be translated into an early clinical drug product.

nodes.title: Plasma Protein Binding and Brain Unbound Fraction
nodes.content: Future DMPK experiment. Quantify plasma protein binding across human, rat, and dog and determine unbound fraction in brain homogenate or relevant CNS matrix. The purpose is to translate total exposure into pharmacologically meaningful unbound exposure and to improve dose projection, safety margin calculation, and interpretation of CNS efficacy and toxicology.

nodes.title: Hepatocyte Stability, Metabolite ID, and Reaction Phenotyping
nodes.content: Future DMPK experiment. Run human, rat, and dog hepatocyte and microsome studies to estimate intrinsic clearance, identify major metabolites, and determine which CYPs or non-CYP enzymes drive turnover. The work should also assess whether any human-predominant metabolite may later require additional qualification and whether the observed rat PK in the paper is compatible with a clinically useful oral dosing regimen.

nodes.title: Caco-2 and MDR1/BCRP Transporter Assessment
nodes.content: Future DMPK experiment. Measure passive permeability, efflux ratio, and transporter sensitivity in a cell-based permeability system to determine whether oral absorption or brain penetration may be limited by active efflux. Because the paper showed strong brain exposure in rats, this experiment helps determine whether the same may hold across species and whether formulation or medicinal chemistry mitigation would be needed before clinic.

nodes.title: Human Exposure Projection and Tox Species Selection
nodes.content: Future translational experiment. Integrate PK, brain penetration, unbound exposure, metabolism, route, and formulation data to choose the definitive rodent and non-rodent toxicology species and to derive a scientifically defensible first-in-human starting dose strategy. The output should be a written exposure-based rationale linking anticipated human pharmacology to nonclinical no-observed-adverse-effect levels and safety margins.

nodes.title: GLP Ames Confirmation Study
nodes.content: Future regulatory toxicology experiment. Repeat bacterial reverse mutation testing under a GLP-compliant framework using qualified test article and finalized reporting. Although a non-GLP Ames screen was already completed in the paper, an IND package should rely on a formal GLP or GLP-like study package acceptable for regulatory review and consistent with the final development material.

nodes.title: GLP In Vitro Micronucleus Assay
nodes.content: Future regulatory toxicology experiment. Conduct an in vitro micronucleus study under GLP conditions using the final selected test article to complete the core genotoxicity battery expected before first-in-human dosing. The study should be designed with adequate concentration selection, metabolic activation conditions, and exposure characterization so that any signal can be interpreted relative to exposure and cytotoxicity.

nodes.title: Validated Bioanalytical Methods for PK and TK
nodes.content: Future bioanalysis experiment. Validate quantitative LC-MS/MS methods for 11h and key analytes in rat and dog plasma, and in any tissue matrices needed to interpret toxicology or bridging studies. The methods must support GLP toxicokinetic sampling, stability handling, incurred sample reproducibility expectations, and reliable comparison back to the exploratory PK data already generated in the paper.

nodes.title: 14-Day Rat Dose-Range Finding Tox and TK
nodes.content: Future non-GLP toxicology experiment. Conduct a short repeat-dose oral study in rats using the intended tox formulation, collecting clinical observations, body weight, food consumption, clinical pathology, toxicokinetics, and gross or limited histopathology as needed. The goal is to identify a feasible high dose, target organs, reversibility considerations, and a dose range suitable for the later GLP 28-day study.

nodes.title: 14-Day Dog Dose-Range Finding Tox and TK
nodes.content: Future non-rodent toxicology experiment. Conduct the companion range-finding study in dogs, using the intended oral formulation and collecting the same core endpoints as in the rat study, including TK. The outcome should define dose selection and monitoring priorities for the definitive GLP non-rodent repeat-dose study and reveal whether emesis, GI intolerance, or CNS effects emerge in a species more clinically informative than rodents.

nodes.title: Core Safety Pharmacology CNS Battery
nodes.content: Future safety pharmacology experiment. Run a focused CNS safety battery in the definitive species or in a justified supporting model to assess locomotor activity, functional observational battery endpoints, body temperature, and neurologic signs at exposures bracketing projected clinical levels. The purpose is to convert the favorable exploratory behavioral profile in the paper into a more regulatory-facing CNS safety assessment.

nodes.title: Core Safety Pharmacology Respiratory Battery
nodes.content: Future safety pharmacology experiment. Measure respiratory rate, tidal volume, minute volume, and related parameters after oral dosing in an appropriate nonclinical system. The goal is to exclude major acute respiratory liabilities and complete the core safety pharmacology triad expected around IND-enabling development.

nodes.title: Cardiovascular Telemetry Safety Pharmacology
nodes.content: Future safety pharmacology experiment. Run an in vivo cardiovascular telemetry study in a justified non-rodent species to monitor heart rate, blood pressure, ECG intervals, and other cardiovascular signals at clinically relevant and supra-therapeutic exposures. This is needed because the in vitro ion channel panel in the paper is encouraging but does not replace an integrated in vivo cardiovascular safety assessment for IND support.

nodes.title: GMP API Manufacture and Release Testing
nodes.content: Future CMC experiment. Manufacture a GMP or at minimum tox-supporting late-phase API batch of 11h using the selected scalable route, release it with qualified specifications, and document impurity controls, residual solvent profile, and batch genealogy. This node is a critical bridge between exploratory paper data and material acceptable for definitive toxicology and the IND CMC section.

nodes.title: Clinical and Tox Drug Product Manufacture
nodes.content: Future CMC experiment. Convert the selected API form into a defined oral drug product or toxicology formulation package, complete release testing, and generate the material that will be dosed in definitive GLP studies and described in the IND. The work should include process description, container closure, dose strength rationale, and comparability to earlier exploratory formulations where relevant.

nodes.title: API and Drug Product Stability Program
nodes.content: Future CMC experiment. Initiate accelerated and long-term stability on the selected API and drug product configurations, including appearance, potency, degradants, and physical performance attributes needed for release and clinical shelf-life justification. For the initial IND, the package must provide enough real-time and accelerated stability data to support the proposed retest period and clinical use window.

nodes.title: GLP 28-Day Rat Toxicology with TK and Histopathology
nodes.content: Future definitive toxicology experiment. Conduct a GLP repeat-dose oral toxicology study in rat with toxicokinetic sampling, full clinical pathology, necropsy, organ weights, and histopathology sufficient to identify target organs and derive a no-observed-adverse-effect level or equivalent regulatory point of departure. This is one of the core studies gating IND readiness.

nodes.title: GLP 28-Day Dog Toxicology with TK and Histopathology
nodes.content: Future definitive non-rodent toxicology experiment. Conduct the companion GLP repeat-dose oral study in dog with TK, clinical pathology, cardiovascular and GI monitoring as appropriate, necropsy, and histopathology. Particular attention should be paid to class-related tolerability liabilities, including emesis and GI effects, because the exploratory ferret data in the paper is encouraging but not itself regulatory-enabling.

nodes.title: Pre-IND Briefing Package
nodes.content: Future regulatory node. Prepare a focused pre-IND package summarizing pharmacology, DMPK, preliminary toxicology, CMC plan, proposed indication, and the clinical entry strategy, with specific FDA questions on adequacy of species selection, genotox package, safety pharmacology timing, formulation comparability, and first-in-human dose rationale. This node is about de-risking the actual IND package before all definitive studies finish.

nodes.title: FDA Pre-IND Meeting and Action Item Closure
nodes.content: Future regulatory node. Hold the pre-IND interaction, capture written minutes, and convert agency feedback into concrete program decisions on study design, reporting expectations, and any package deficiencies that must be closed before filing. This node often changes the critical path because it can confirm that some studies are sufficient as designed while forcing redesign of others.

nodes.title: Investigator Brochure and Nonclinical Summary
nodes.content: Future authoring node. Draft the investigator brochure, integrated nonclinical summary, and source tables that consolidate the paper findings with all later IND-enabling work. The document should be internally consistent on formulation history, exposure metrics, safety interpretation, and the rationale for the proposed first-in-human study population and monitoring plan.

nodes.title: First-in-Human SAD Protocol and Starting Dose Rationale
nodes.content: Future clinical planning node. Write the first-in-human single-ascending-dose protocol, including inclusion criteria, stopping rules, safety monitoring, food effect positioning if relevant, PK/PD endpoints, and the exposure-based starting dose rationale tied back to the definitive nonclinical margins. This node translates the IND-enabling package into a concrete clinical plan.

nodes.title: IND Dossier Authoring and eCTD Assembly
nodes.content: Future final integration node. Assemble the CMC, pharmacology, toxicology, and clinical sections into the IND, reconcile tables and batch histories, verify all source reports are final and signed, and build the submission-ready eCTD sequence. The work should explicitly trace how the exploratory paper results informed candidate selection but distinguish those early studies from the definitive IND-enabling package.

nodes.title: IND Ready to File
nodes.content: Program milestone. All definitive nonclinical reports, CMC release and stability support, investigator brochure, and first-in-human protocol are complete and internally consistent; the dossier has been quality-checked and is ready for submission. This node marks the transition from preclinical development into regulatory filing execution.
